title: Lift Basket Balance
desc: One of Mount Pillar's cargo lifts is rising crooked, which is how crates start falling on innocent workers.

steps.text:
Foot traffic uses Mount Pillar's ramps. Bulk cargo uses lift baskets, chitin-framed platforms on rope winches that ride the central shaft between levels. They creak constantly, which everyone accepts. Today one of them is listing badly enough to scrape the guide rail on ascent, which no one accepts at all.

The winch master demonstrates the problem by sending an empty basket upward. It climbs straight for ten feet, then drifts and kisses the stone hard enough to leave a white streak on the side frame. "Either the counterweight stack is wrong, or the outgoing loads are being packed like a dare," he says. "Check the weights below, or sort the next cargo run and rebalance the basket before someone loses a crate down the shaft."

options.label: Inspect the counterweight chamber below the shaft
Sort the outgoing cargo and rebalance the basket

steps.text:
The counterweight chamber sits beneath the lift shaft in a slot of cool stone, where dressed blocks rise and fall behind a barrier frame as the baskets travel above. Up close the problem is obvious. One edge block in the weight stack has chipped at the corner and started seating crooked, which throws the whole column just far enough out of line to tug the basket sideways on ascent.

You help the winch master unload the bad stone, roll in a replacement from the spare rack, and reset the stack so each block sits flush against the guide grooves. When he sends the empty basket up again, it climbs clean and quiet, no sideways drift, no rail scrape, only the steady wooden complaint of a machine doing its job. The winch master watches it rise until it vanishes into the shaft. "Good," he says. "A proper lift should sound impatient, not dangerous."

options.label: Log the replaced block and clear the lift for service

steps.text:
The next outgoing run is stacked for three levels at once: sealed fungus flour, two bundles of cut root, tool crates for a maintenance gang, and a tray of ceramic jars someone had the poor judgment to place on the outer edge. You restack the whole load by weight and destination, heavy goods low and central, fragile pieces tied inward, nothing allowed to lean toward the shaft wall no matter how much it would like the extra space.

It is slower work than everyone nearby wants. The carriers keep hovering, the winch master keeps checking the lane, and the evening cargo line keeps lengthening behind you. But once the basket is tied down and sent upward, it rises straight from the first pull and tracks between the rails without a scrape. The carriers waiting below stop grumbling, which is the closest thing a loading bay gets to praise. The winch master taps the now-empty platform with one knuckle. "There. Turns out gravity is much easier to work with when you stop arguing with it."

options.label: Wave the next load forward and keep the lift moving
